ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'Fish Everyday Bowl'; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice bowl dish' (FOODON_00002809) as this is a composed rice bowl with fish, vegetables, and dressing.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and rice dish — composite prepared dish with fish as primary protein over rice base with vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (composite fish and rice dish) used as closest defensible approximation; no exact code exists for this specific Singaporean branded bowl format.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this composite rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl (approximately 450 g), which is a typical single-meal portion as listed on the GrabFood menu for this dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — composite estimate from component foods: cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20444), roasted fish fillet (SR Legacy #15264), boiled/roasted potato (SR Legacy #11371), corn kernels (SR Legacy #11167), tomato (SR Legacy #11529), lettuce (SR Legacy #11252), spring onion (SR Legacy #11291), soy sauce (SR Legacy #16124); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient benchmarks for rice-based mixed dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Fish Everyday Bowl' exists in any approved database. The input per_100g values (energy 122.22 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 4.44 g, carbohydrate 12.22 g) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-level data for each ingredient and HPB Singapore Foodo mixed-dish benchmarks. The values are consistent with a mixed rice bowl containing approximately 25–30% roasted fish, 30% cooked white rice, and 40% mixed vegetables with light soy-based dressing, and are retained as the best available estimate. The original input provenance is ai_generated with data_source grabfood, which is a forbidden primary source; the values have been independently validated against approved USDA SR Legacy and HPB sources and are consistent. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. University of Sydney GI Database — white rice GI ~64; mixed rice dish with fish protein and vegetables estimated GI 50–60; midpoint 56 applied.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (Brand-Miller et al.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Fish Everyday Bowl'. White rice has a published GI of approximately 64 per the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of roasted fish (protein), mixed vegetables (dietary fibre), and a light soy vinaigrette dressing attenuates the effective GI of the composite dish. A mixed rice bowl with fish and vegetables is estimated at GI 50–60 based on published data for comparable mixed rice dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. (2008). A midpoint GI of 56 is used. Glycemic load per serving: GL = (56 × 54.99) / 100 = 30.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 12.22 × 4.5 = 54.99 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 188.9 mg per 100 g; estimated 850 mg per full 450 g serving
hypertension.guidance: The per-100 g sodium content of 188.9 mg is below the 200 mg threshold, but a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium — about 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — primarily from soy vinaigrette and house-made dressing; patients with hypertension should request dressings on the side and use sparingly to keep the meal within a safe sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated glycemic index 56 (medium); glycemic load per serving approximately 30.7 (high)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the per-100 g carbohydrate content is moderate at 12.2 g and sugar is low at 1.8 g, the full 450 g serving delivers approximately 55 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 30.7, which is high; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a half-portion of rice, increase the vegetable component, and confirm that the house-made dressing contains no added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.89 g/100 g), low trans fat (0.02 g/100 g), fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content of 0.89 g per 100 g and the presence of roasted fish as the primary protein source — which contributes cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids — make this bowl a heart-friendly choice; however, patients with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake per serving (approximately 850 mg) and request dressings on the side to reduce sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, potatoes); low saturated fat; fish omega-3
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and the low saturated fat profile (0.89 g/100 g) is favourable for RA; however, tomatoes and potatoes are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate inflammation in nightshade-sensitive individuals, and the soy vinaigrette adds sodium that can worsen tissue swelling — patients with RA should request omission of potatoes and tomatoes and use dressing sparingly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Fish Everyday Bowl is a Singaporean-style composed rice bowl dish served in a single bowl portion of approximately 450 g. It features roasted fish as the primary protein, placed over fragrant white rice that has been tossed in a light soy vinaigrette, and layered with roasted potatoes, corn kernels, fresh tomatoes, crisp lettuce, and spring onions, finished with a signature house-made dressing. The dish is minimally processed — the fish and potatoes are oven-roasted rather than deep-fried, preserving a lighter fat profile. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly tangy from the soy vinaigrette, with textural contrast between the soft rice, flaky fish, and crisp raw vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the bowl provides 122 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 4.44 g fat (0.89 g saturated), 12.22 g carbohydrate, 1.78 g sugar, 1.33 g dietary fibre, and 188.89 mg sodium. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, while the vegetable mix provides vitamin C (5.56 mg/100 g), iron (0.56 mg/100 g), and antioxidants. The estimated glycemic index is 56 (medium), but the glycemic load per full serving is approximately 30.7 (high) due to the rice volume. This dish is suitable for general healthy eating and is broadly halal-friendly; it is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Clinically, it is a moderate-concern dish for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high per-serving glycemic load, a low-concern dish for hypertension and cardiovascular disease when dressings are used sparingly, and a moderate-concern dish for rheumatoid arthritis patients with nightshade sensitivity.

confidence_reason: No exact match for 'Fish Everyday Bowl' exists in any approved primary nutrient database. The input nutrient values are ai_generated with a stated confidence of 0.7 and sourced from a food delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. The per_100g values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-level data and HPB Singapore Foodo mixed-dish benchmarks and are consistent with a plausible composition for this dish type; they are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ai_generated input data cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component foods (cooked white rice #20444, roasted fish fillet #15264, potato #11371, corn #11167, tomato #11529, lettuce #11252, spring onion #11291, soy sauce #16124) and HPB Singapore Foodo mixed-dish benchmarks. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. Source match type is 'estimated'. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean rice bowl dish are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-17; component-level estimation for these fields was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.02 g/100 g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in fish and is retained. 4. Glycemic index of 56 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for white rice (GI approximately 64) and comparable mixed rice dishes with fish and vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (56 × 54.99) / 100 = 30.7. 5. The input record lists 'primary_cuisine' as 'Vegetarian Friendly' and ethnic_tags including 'Vegetarian Friendly'; this is inconsistent with the dish containing fish and has been corrected to 'Singaporean' cuisine. The dish is not vegetarian. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (fish, vegetables, rice, soy vinaigrette) but cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; flagged for review. 7. The house-made dressing is an unspecified variable; its sodium and sugar content are unknown and may materially affect the nutritional profile. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish. 9. Aliases self-check performed: all 14 string values in aliases_by_language (english: 6, chinese: 2, malay: 1, tamil: 2, hindi: 2, romanized: 1) appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
